content: The single most common mistake beginners make when building an HTTP/1.1 parser from scratch is assuming that a simple string split or regex-based approach will suffice, which leads to catastrophic failure under real-world traffic. You will encounter requests with leading whitespace, carriage returns embedded in headers, missing CRLF delimiters between lines, and malformed request lines where the method or URI length violates specification bounds. A naive implementation using `String::split('\n')` immediately breaks because it does not validate the strict two-character sequence requirement of LF (0x0A) for line terminators; HTTP/1.1 mandates that a CR-LF pair must terminate every header and request line, yet many clients or proxies send malformed data to test robustness. If your parser ignores this, you will inadvertently accept garbage as valid headers, causing security vulnerabilities like buffer overflows when parsing chunked payloads. The correct approach requires manual byte-by-byte iteration through the input buffer rather than relying on high-level string operations; you must track the state machine explicitly to distinguish between a request line, header section, and optional chunked body. When implementing request line parsing, you cannot simply parse until you hit whitespace because an empty URI or missing method is invalid per RFC 7230; your parser must enforce that the method is one of GET, POST, PUT, etc., followed by a non-empty URI, then optionally query parameters and fragment identifiers separated by `?` and `#`. Header parsing introduces another layer of complexity: you cannot assume headers end after the first empty line because some implementations omit the final blank line; instead, you must detect EOF or an explicit CRLF sequence to terminate the header section while handling cases where a single trailing newline exists but no data follows. Chunked encoding is particularly treacherous for beginners who forget that the chunk size and extension must both be followed by two CR-LF characters before the next chunk begins; if your logic skips this validation, you will misalign the pointer and read into subsequent headers or terminate prematurely. Pipelining adds another dimension of statefulness: your parser cannot simply parse request lines sequentially because it must buffer incomplete requests until all headers are received to determine whether to apply a 100 (Continue) response or wait for completion; this requires buffering raw bytes in a `Vec<u8>` and processing them as a stream, not line-by-line. Finally, compliance demands that you reject requests where the total header length exceeds a reasonable limit or where chunked boundaries are misaligned by even a single byte, which often trips up parsers designed with regex patterns instead of strict state machines. The lesson is clear: never trust external formatting utilities for protocol parsing; implement a deterministic finite automaton in Rust using `enum` variants to represent current states (REQUEST_LINE, HEADERS, CHUNKED_BODY) and transition only when exact byte sequences match the specification, ensuring your parser rejects invalid input immediately rather than silently corrupting data.
